revision_summary: Stage 3 has a fundamental, call-wide speaker-label inversion error that invalidates the majority of its analysis. The transcript labels are inverted from the standard setter/prospect convention: the speaker tagged 'customer' is Carter, the Ion setter (he identifies himself as 'Carter calling from Ion' at 00:07). The speaker tagged 'rep' is Michael, the actual prospect (he is the one who has been texting multiple solar companies for pricing). Every Stage 3 section that analyzes Michael's lines as setter behavior and Carter's lines as customer behavior is reading the call backwards. Specific consequences of this error that require full rerun: (1) rep_intent section analyzes Michael's consumer shopping behavior as if it were a setter's strategic failure; (2) cherry_pick_moments[1] raises a conduct-issue hypothesis (rep using company dialer for personal inquiry) that is factually impossible once labels are corrected; (3) cherry_pick_moments[3] flags a cross-sell miss against Carter's own explanation of Ion's process, not a prospect signal; (4) coaching_focus[0] coaches Michael (the prospect) on call-prep discipline; (5) outcome_dispute recommends 'wrong party / internal misdial' when the call is a legitimate pipeline callback. The correct reading of this call: Carter (Ion setter) received a system-generated callback trigger for Michael, a prospect already texting Ion's Alex. Carter did not have CRM context before the call connected, put Michael on hold for ~86 seconds to look up his record, confirmed Michael was already in the pipeline with Alex, and cleanly routed him back to Alex. Real coaching gaps to surface on rerun: (a) Carter's lack of pre-call CRM visibility causing an unnecessary hold; (b) Carter's re-entry after the hold lacking a hold acknowledgment; (c) absence of a codex-defined callback/re-engagement intro variant for this scenario; (d) Carter's intro not explaining why Ion's system called Michael, creating the initial confusion. The outcome tag should be revised from 'wrong party / internal misdial' to reflect a legitimate pipeline-routing call with no appointment needed.
